<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: asi-is-a-stack-not-a-model
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/asi-is-a-stack-not-a-model.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/asi-is-a-stack-not-a-model.qmd@d16c2720
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# ASI Is a Stack, Not a Model

The book begins by changing the object in view. The subject is not a magic
model, a prompt wrapper, or an agent loop with a grand name. It is a governed
system whose parts must say what they own, what they may do, what evidence
they create, and how they can be stopped, repaired, or replaced.

That opening move matters because the rest of the architecture adds machinery
quickly. Planning, memory, routing, proof, execution, compression, governance,
and recursive improvement can sound like one impressive cloud of capability if
their boundaries are not visible. The stack frame prevents that blur. It asks
every mechanism the same questions: what responsibility do you own, what
artifact do you pass, what authority ceiling binds you, what evidence state
can you honestly create, and what failure mode should stop you?

A model can generate, compress, criticize, route, and propose. Those abilities
are powerful, but they are not the whole institution. A governed stack decides
when a model output becomes a plan, when a plan becomes a job, when a job may
touch a tool, when a tool result becomes evidence, and when evidence can
justify replacement. Without that separation, intelligence becomes hard to
audit at exactly the moment it becomes important.

The first claim of the book is therefore modest but load-bearing: efficient
ASI should be modeled as a governed stack of cooperating layers rather than as
one undifferentiated model. The claim is architectural. It remains at
`argument` support. The point is not that this repository already contains a
working ASI stack; the point is that every later chapter needs this unit of
analysis before it can make a disciplined claim.

## Problem

Advanced AI systems that plan, remember, verify, act, route work, compress
representations, and improve under governance need a single architecture
frame. Otherwise each paper, component, and prototype can sound like it is
competing for the center of the system. One source emphasizes intent to
execution. Another emphasizes stateful cognition. Another emphasizes
verification, labor, orchestration, or stable capability replacement. Read
without an architecture, those ideas become an anthology. Read through a stack
contract, they become fragments of one machine.

The first boundary is conceptual. An ASI system should not be described only
by the model that generates tokens, the agent loop that calls tools, or the
benchmark score that reports progress. Those are components and measurements.
The architecture is the set of contracts that decides how intent becomes work,
how memory becomes context, how reasoning becomes claims, how claims become
authority, how authority becomes execution, and how execution becomes evidence
for future change.

This is why the unit of analysis has to be fixed before the book starts adding
layers. The unit is not "the model." The unit is a governed stack of
cooperating layers. Each layer has a responsibility, an interface, a durable
artifact, an invariant, a failure mode, and a promotion rule. Later layers can
add detail only because the stack frame says what it means for those details
to compose.

The frame also gives the reader a practical test. A new mechanism belongs in
the stack only when it lowers confusion at a boundary: who owns the decision,
what artifact crosses the interface, which authority is available, which
evidence state is possible, and what rollback path exists if the mechanism
fails. If it cannot answer those questions, it may still be an idea, but it is
not yet an architectural layer.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A larger model does not by itself define authority. A prompt wrapper does not
define evidence. An agent loop does not define memory discipline, tool
permissions, source sufficiency, rollback, or replacement safety.

The missing piece is system accountability. A fluent model can propose a plan
without owning the authority to execute it. A retrieval system can surface a
source without proving that the source is current, permitted, or sufficient
for the task. A router can choose a specialist without preserving the residual
burden created by that choice. A benchmark can improve while hiding new
failure cases. These failures appear at interfaces, so they need interface
records.

The ASI Stack does not reject powerful models. It places them. A model may be
a generator, compressor, critic, planner, router, simulator, or verifier. It
should not silently become the constitution, the memory authority, the
deployment authority, the approval body, and the evidence registry at the same
time.

The stack frame also sits near established architecture traditions rather than
outside them. MRKL-style systems decompose language models with expert modules.
LLM-agent surveys organize agents around memory, planning, and action. The
Standard Model of the Mind supplies cognitive-architecture lineage. Layered
robot-control work shows the older idea that intelligent behavior can be
organized as interacting layers rather than one monolithic controller. These
sources are comparators. They do not validate the ASI Stack, but they help
name the adjacent territory.

The ASI Stack adds a particular emphasis: governance, authority ceilings,
claim states, proof hooks, rollback paths, and recursive-improvement
boundaries are not decorations around intelligence. They are part of the
architecture.

## Core Claim

Efficient ASI should be modeled as a governed stack of cooperating layers
rather than as one undifferentiated model (evidence boundary: architectural
argument).

The live book keeps this claim at `argument` support. VIEA supports the
intent-to-execution spine, BeastBrain supports the local stateful stack
vocabulary, Aletheia supports the trusted-output and verification boundary,
Talos supports the labor and execution layer, SCF supports governed
replacement, and MoECOT remains implementation-reference context rather than
reproduced evidence. External architecture sources ground the surrounding
vocabulary for modular systems, LLM agents, cognitive architectures, and
layered control, but they do not reproduce or validate the ASI Stack.

The argument is that decomposition is both a governance device and an
efficiency device. A stack can route the smallest adequate capability to a
subtask, preserve context only where it is useful, compile repeated work into
tools, and test the exact boundary that changed. A monolith can still be
powerful, but it has fewer places to attach evidence, rollback, permissions,
and replacement rules.

## Stack Map

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  Constitution["Alignment and constitution"]
  Governance["Governance and authority"]
  Intent["Intent and command contracts"]
  Planning["Planning and semantic IR"]
  Memory["Virtual context memory"]
  Reasoning["Claims, belief revision, and verification"]
  Execution["Labor OS, tools, and runtime adapters"]
  Routing["Routing and specialist capability fields"]
  Compression["Compression, representation, and residuals"]
  Evidence["Evidence, tests, proofs, and benchmarks"]
  Improvement["Governed self-improvement"]

  Constitution --> Governance
  Governance --> Intent
  Intent --> Planning
  Planning --> Memory
  Memory --> Reasoning
  Reasoning --> Execution
  Planning --> Routing
  Routing --> Compression
  Execution --> Evidence
  Compression --> Evidence
  Evidence --> Improvement
  Improvement --> Governance

  Governance -. "authority ceilings" .-> Execution
  Evidence -. "qualification and rollback" .-> Routing
  Memory -. "source-bound context" .-> Reasoning
```

Read the map as a responsibility loop, not a capability ladder.
Self-improvement is not outside the stack. It returns to governance, where new
capabilities must be qualified, bounded, and made reversible before they
become defaults.

## Mechanism

The stack frame refuses to let any one component inherit every role. A layer
contract is the mechanism that makes that refusal concrete. The artifact is a
Layer Boundary Record, and a layer is mature only when it can answer a stable
set of questions.

What lifecycle state is the layer in? Who owns the boundary? Which chapters
currently carry it? What responsibility does it own? What artifact does it
emit? How does handoff work? What authority does it have, and what authority
does it lack? What invariant must survive implementation changes? What
evidence would justify promotion, replacement, or rollback?

Those questions are the book's defense against anthology drift. Each source
can introduce a mechanism, but the mechanism enters the stack only by naming
its responsibility, interface, artifact, invariant, failure mode, evidence
gate, and non-claim. Source queues keep future writing runs context-loaded.
Evidence states keep clearer prose from becoming stronger support without the
corresponding artifact.

The raw LLM sits inside that contract as a powerful semantic machine. It can
compress, expand, draft, classify, translate, route, critique, and propose
repairs. The stack around it decides when those operations count as evidence,
when they require verification, when they are only suggestions, and when they
may trigger external action. The model can move meaning; the stack decides
when that movement becomes a claim, job, permission, artifact, or release.

The living-book workflow mirrors the architecture. Source queues prevent
context drift. Claim states prevent evidence inflation. Proof targets prevent
vague formalism. Render and validation gates prevent the public book from
drifting away from the repository that defines it.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Source idea or implementation pressure"] --> B["Assign owner layer"]
  B --> C["Define interface artifact"]
  C --> D["Declare authority ceiling"]
  D --> E["Name invariant and failure mode"]
  E --> F["Attach evidence gate"]
  F --> G{"Validated surface exists?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Candidate for promotion"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Design rationale or residual"]
  H --> J["Regression, proof, schema, or runtime trace"]
  I --> F
```

This lifecycle is intentionally conservative. It lets the book absorb new
papers, code, diagrams, proofs, and source notes without renumbering the
architecture around every artifact. The stable object is the contract. Prose
can improve, chapters can move, and implementations can be replaced, but the
contract must continue to say what the layer owns and what it is not allowed
to claim.

## Interfaces

Layer boundaries become usable through the Layer Boundary Record. At minimum,
that record names the layer id, lifecycle state, owner, responsibility,
chapter references, traceability state, input artifacts, output artifacts,
authority ceiling, handoff protocol, contract references, change policy,
integration decision, owned invariants, failure modes, evidence gates,
downstream interfaces, promotion blockers, source references, support-state
effect, and non-claims.

The upstream interface accepts human purposes, institutional constraints,
safety requirements, and explicit authority grants. The downstream interface
emits stable obligations, boundaries, evidence labels, and governance
constraints. If an adjacent layer cannot identify its input artifact or
authority ceiling, the right behavior is not improvisation. It is a
missing-contract record.

This interface is also what makes a living book structurally dynamic. A new
chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant,
artifact type, or failure mode. Otherwise, the material should merge into the
layer that already owns the boundary. The book should be edited like a stack,
not like a table of contents.

When a future paper arrives, the first question is not "does this deserve a
chapter?" The first question is "which contract does this change?" If it
changes no contract, it may become a source note, example, caveat, or appendix
entry. If it changes a contract, the outline, chapter, schema, proof target,
and crosswalk should move together.

## Invariants

- Layer boundaries remain explicit.
- Claims carry support states.
- Reasoning ability never implies execution authority.
- Improvement routes back through governance instead of bypassing it.
- New capability evidence enters through layer contracts rather than narrative
  enthusiasm.

The central invariant is separability. Planning, memory, reasoning, execution,
routing, compression, evidence, and governance may cooperate, but none of them
may silently absorb the authority or evidence duties of another layer. A model
output can propose; it does not execute by being confident. A benchmark can
inform; it does not authorize deployment by being high. A proof can certify a
finite predicate; it does not imply broad safety. This separation lets the
architecture inspect a failure without rewriting the whole system.

## Failure modes

The first failure is anthology drift: the book becomes a catalog of
interesting ideas instead of one architecture. The second is monolith drift:
the architecture starts attributing planning, memory, proof, execution, and
governance to one opaque model again.

Evidence inflation is the third failure. A design rationale, source note,
local proof, or benchmark-shaped intention starts sounding like an implemented
result. Authority inflation is its partner: execution authority, deployment
authority, and evidence authority become ambient properties of "the model."

The practical result is loss of addressability. No one can say which layer
owns the claim, which artifact would test it, which boundary would stop it, or
which residual remains. The stack frame exists to make that loss visible early
enough to repair.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A useful first build is a stack map, a layer boundary schema, one valid
fixture, a generated source map, and Appendix C. That bundle is not evidence
that the architecture runs. It gives the rest of the book a typed surface for
saying which layer owns which lifecycle state, owner, chapter references,
traceability state, responsibility, artifact, handoff protocol, contract
references, authority ceiling, invariant, integration decision, promotion
blockers, source references, support-state effect, and non-claim.

The repository now also has a stack-layer traceability audit that checks the
public layer-boundary fixture, the opener's six source mappings, source-to-layer
visibility, claim labels, support states, and no-promotion markers. That is
evidence of repository discipline, not evidence that a deployed stack exists.

A passing boundary record should include at least one blocked integration
path: a reasoning layer trying to execute, an evidence layer trying to promote
without artifacts, or a routing layer trying to widen authority. That makes
the stack boundary test visible from the beginning.

The minimum is complete only when a future editor can add a layer without
renumbering chapters, hiding ownership, or silently changing support state.

## Beyond the State of the Art

A finished ASI stack is not a larger model, a more elaborate agent loop, or a
compound-AI toolkit with a nicer diagram. Scale can improve a component, but
weights do not carry authority ceilings or rollback ownership. Agent loops
can organize tool use, but a loop is not a governed institution unless every
action path preserves permission, evidence, residuals, and refusal. Modular
systems show that decomposition matters, but decomposition alone does not
create claim ledgers, replacement transactions, or recursive-improvement
boundaries.

In the mature reference architecture, alignment and governance constrain every
downstream layer. Planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing,
compression, and evidence exchange typed artifacts rather than hidden state.
Recursive improvement becomes a governed transition with records, gates, and
rollback paths rather than an ambient property of intelligence. Source queues
and evidence states keep future writing runs from turning the book back into
an anthology or allowing polished synthesis to outpace support.

The mature stack absorbs what those other frames get right while making their
missing contracts explicit. Model weights become one replaceable component.
Agentic planning becomes a bounded planning layer. Tools and experts become
routed capabilities with authority ceilings and evidence gates. Every layer
has to name its responsibility, artifact, invariant, failure mode, promotion
rule, non-claims, and rollback path before the reader is asked to trust it.

This stack doctrine is still an architectural claim. It stays at `argument`
support until layer-boundary records, handoff-denial fixtures, traceability
audits over concrete integrations, and governed replacement examples show that
stack separation survives real integration pressure.

## Summary

The book starts with a boundary, not a prediction. A model can be powerful
without being the architecture. A governed stack is the architecture: the
contracts that decide when meaning becomes a plan, when a plan becomes work,
when work becomes evidence, and when evidence can justify change.

That stance changes how capability should be measured. A planning trace needs
an authority boundary. A context packet needs an adequacy test. A routing
decision needs residual accounting. A replacement needs rollback. A proof
needs a precise scope. A release needs a record. Those questions are
unavailable when intelligence is treated as one opaque model behavior.

This does not prove that the architecture is implemented or safe. It gives the
rest of the book a stable question to ask of every powerful mechanism: what
does this part own, what evidence does it create, what can stop it, and how
would the system know if it failed?

## Handoff

With the stack boundary established, The Efficient ASI Hypothesis asks how
capability should grow without turning scale into the only design variable.
Architecture identity now has to become architecture economics: if
intelligence is a stack of governed layers, then efficiency means routing,
reuse, compression, verification, and residual accounting at the correct layer
rather than spending maximum cognition everywhere.
